posts.name: p@ulbortin

It looks like I’m having a virtually monogynious relationship with M Charmes.

Ed Gonzales:
Jeremy Holmes:

I like Meursault-Charmes, I love Meursault-Perrieres!

^

Of course MP is the Grand Cru candidate but Meursualt Charmes can be great and it is never almost never talked about. Besides H. Boillot and the probably overpriced Roulot versions who else makes great Charmes? Anybody have memorable bottles?

posts.name: Robert Thornton
posts.cooked:
I like the idea here, Perrieres gets all the credit (not without reason), but there are a ton of other great Meursault vineyards. After Perrieres, I’m maybe more of a Poruzots or Genevrieres guy, but love Charmes too.

Yes, I love Roulot and Boillot, but Francois Jobard is up there too. Can be great. I’m a big Matrot fan. Again, another great Perrieres producer, but the Charmes is great too. Of course Lafon. Boyer-Martenot has fans, I’ve really liked some of them, never fallen in love and am worried premox-wise (as I sadly am with Matrot too, whom I love), but apparently can be a great Charmes. And I think Colin-Morey makes one too. I haven’t had it but have liked other wines he’s made so I’d go for that.

There are others too, fortunately for Charmes lovers, many producers get to make it.

Cheers,
-Robert

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Paul,
I think Bouchard make a really solid Charmes. Lafon’s is brilliant but at the same price point as Roulot. Buisson-Charles are a good source at the right price and the small sample set I’ve tasted from Pierre-Yves Colin-Morey have been very good.
Best Regards
Jeremy

posts.cooked: recently had a 2013 PYCM (Pierre Yves Colin Morey) Meursault Charmes that was excellent

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I suspect that the stereotype of Charmes as ‘fat’ (which it certainly can be) has a lot to do with its comparative unpopularity—especially vis a vis Perrieres. I think the same is true of Batard Montrachet. It is also the largest 1er cru in Meursault and quite variable (divided into two lieux-dits as is well known). I know several seasoned tasters, including one of some prominence in the UK wine trade, who think that Lafon’s Charmes, rather than his Perrieres, is the best Meursault in his cellar (perhaps because it has tended to be less worked than the Perrieres and the Montrachet?) and I am personally tentatively inclined to agree. The handful of old Lafon Charmes that I’ve encountered are my personal benchmarks for the vineyard—most recently 1979 earlier this year was just lovely. I’d love to try more.

The Buisson-Charles rendition does indeed seem to be very serious and I’m looking forward to seeing how the wines develop.

posts.cooked: Pierre yves Colin morey has an insanely good charmes. I think it’s #2 behind Roulot for my tastes. He gets so much acidity and energy out of the vineyard

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked: I opened a '12 Jadot Meursault Charmes tonight out of .375 ($18.99 from PC!). It seriously exceeded expectations, with impressive density and freshness. Not Roulot, of course, but quite nice.

posts.name: Bob Cuozzi
posts.cooked: In late 1995 I was about to deploy to Bosnia for a while. We had an opportunity to take a quick bit of leave before I left. We stayed at a nice little B&B in TriBeCa. It was snowing like crazy so we walked through an almost empty lower Manhattan in ever deepening snow to our dinner reservation at Montrachet. There were three other tables occupied. We had the lobster risotto with their last bottle of the 1986 Comte Lafon Mersault Charmes… A great meal, wonderful moment… And easily one of the best wines of any varietal, color you name it I have ever had. Simply perfect. Cheers, Bob

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked: 2010 Jadot Charmes was a very memorable white burg for me.

Jeremy,

If price is the same do you prefer the Roulot Charmes to the Lafon version? And if so why?

I have never tasted the Roulot, but the 2010 Lafon Charmes is one of the best whites I’ve ever had

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Andreas,

Lafon’s version is a lot more pared back and finer than it was in the 90’s. His 2009 for example is one of the most brilliant wines of the vintage for mine and the '10 and '11 are superb. I rate it the equal to Roulot’s version and am happy to buy either when offered it at a fair price.

Cheers
Jeremy

P.S: I also love Henri Germain’s version. A magnum of the 2000 is on my shortlist for white wine of the year.

c fu:

Henri germain makes a beautiful version as well

Only had it twice, but both times were memorable.

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked: Yearly, I am offered a single bottle of 1er cru Lafon either Charmes or Genevrieres from a small shop. Which would you take? (I also get a bottle of clos de la barre.)

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked: Over here and at the prices I am offered, the Roulot Charmes is more than twice as expensive as the Lafon Charmes. Since I really like the Lafon Charmes (hoping that newer vintages will not be as prem-ox affected as older vintages), I buy Lafon.

posts.name: Herwig Janssen
posts.cooked:
In Meursault , Perrieres in king imo . After that , I guess it depends on the wine maker .
I also tasted the 2014 Charmes from chateau de Meursault , a big fat creamy wine from a top white Burgundy year .
Comte Lafon Charmes is very good when not oxidized but usually outshined by his Perrieres and even his Goutte d’Or .
Henri Germain is excellent and a very underestimated wine-maker .
Roulot’s Charmes is very good but his Perrieres is waaaay superior and for the money I would stick
to his villages wines . Actually , Roulot ( who is one of the best white wine-makers ) proves to me that Charmes is not such a fabulous terroir .

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Not yet mentioned here, and outrageously good wines in '13/'14:
Jean Pierre Latour-Giraud.
The '14s are WOW WOW good.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Herwig Janssen:

Roulot’s Charmes is very good but his Perrieres is waaaay superior and for the money I would stick to his villages wines . Actually , Roulot ( who is one of the best white wine-makers ) proves to me that Charmes is not such a fabulous terroir .

Roulot is also pretty candid about the fact that his parcel of Charmes (Dessous!) is not optimally located within a large and heterogeneous vineyard.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Roulot’s lieux dits have always been the best values in their stable, IMO. Lieux dits in Meursault are better known than in any other village’s “villages” wines…deservedly. Many people don’t even realize they aren’t 1er cru in Meursault.

Roulot was the first estate I ever visited in Meursault (in 1988)…and though I was confused by the labels there…I was blown away by the 1986 lieux dits there, even after tasting the 1er crus.
